volumeTitle: Antigone. The Women of Trachis. Philoctetes. Oedipus at Colonus
label: Philoctetes (Section 319 - Section 480)

sections.label: Section 319
sections.original: ἐγὼ δὲ καὐτὸς τοῖσδε μάρτυς ἐν λόγοις ,
sections.translation: And I myself attest your accusations ,

sections.label: Section 320
sections.original: ὡς εἴσʼ ἀληθεῖς οἶδα , συντυχὼν κακῶν
sections.translation: for I know their truth through my own experience with the wickedness of the Atreids and the force of Odysseus .

sections.label: Section 322
sections.original: ἦ γάρ τι καὶ σὺ τοῖς πανωλέθροις ἔχεις
sections.translation: What , do you also have a grievance against the accursed sons of Atreus , a cause for anger at some mistreatment ?

sections.label: Section 324
sections.original: θυμὸν γένοιτο χειρὶ πληρῶσαί ποτε ,
sections.translation: If only I might one day be allowed to fulfill my heart ’ s rage by the deeds of my hand ,

sections.label: Section 325
sections.original: ἵνʼ αἱ Μυκῆναι γνοῖεν ἡ Σπάρτη θʼ ὅτι
sections.translation: so that Mycenae might learn , and Sparta , that Scyros also is a mother of brave men !

sections.label: Section 327
sections.original: εὖ γʼ , ὦ τέκνον · τίνος γὰρ ὧδε τὸν μέγαν
sections.translation: Well said , son ! Now what is the reason that you have come complaining against them with this fierce wrath ?

sections.label: Section 329
sections.original: ὦ παῖ Ποίαντος , ἐξερῶ , μόλις δʼ ἐρῶ ,
sections.translation: I will tell you — and yet it is hard to tell —

sections.label: Section 330
sections.original: ἅγωγʼ ὑπʼ αὐτῶν ἐξελωβήθην μολών .
sections.translation: the outrage that I suffered from them upon my arrival there . For when fate decreed that Achilles should die —

sections.label: Section 332
sections.original: οἴμοι · φράσῃς μοι μὴ πέρα , πρὶν ἂν μάθω
sections.translation: Ah , me ! Tell me no more , until I first know this — is the son of Peleus dead ?

sections.label: Section 334
sections.original: τέθνηκεν , ἀνδρὸς οὐδενός , θεοῦ δʼ ὕπο ,
sections.translation: Dead — not by a mortal hand , but by a god ’ s .

sections.label: Section 335
sections.original: τοξευτός , ὡς λέγουσιν , ἐκ Φοίβου δαμείς .
sections.translation: He was brought down , as men say , by the arrow of Phoebus .

sections.label: Section 336
sections.original: ἀλλʼ εὐγενὴς μὲν ὁ κτανών τε χὠ θανών ·
sections.translation: Well , noble alike are the slayer and the slain . But I am at a loss to know , son , whether I should first inquire into the wrong done you , or mourn the dead .

sections.label: Section 339
sections.original: οἶμαι μὲν ἀρκεῖν σοί γε καὶ τὰ σʼ , ὦ τάλας ,
sections.translation: Your own sorrows , I think , are enough

sections.label: Section 340
sections.original: ἀλγήμαθʼ , ὥστε μὴ τὰ τῶν πέλας στένειν .
sections.translation: for you , unhappy man , without mourning for those of your neighbor .

sections.label: Section 341
sections.original: ὀρθῶς ἔλεξας · τοιγαροῦν τὸ σὸν φράσον
sections.translation: You speak the truth . Therefore tell me again what happened to you , and how they wronged you .

sections.label: Section 343
sections.original: ἦλθόν με νηὶ ποικιλοστόλῳ μέτα
sections.translation: They came for me in a ship elaborately ornamented , shining Odysseus , and he who fostered my father ,

sections.label: Section 345
sections.original: λέγοντες , εἴτʼ ἀληθὲς εἴτʼ ἄρʼ οὖν μάτην ,
sections.translation:
and said — whether truly or falsely , I do not know — that since my father had perished , fate now forbade that anyone but I should take the towers of Troy .
Saying that this , my friend , was how things stood , they caused me no long delay before I set sail in haste ,

sections.label: Section 350
sections.original: μάλιστα μὲν δὴ τοῦ θανόντος ἱμέρῳ ,
sections.translation:
chiefly because of my yearning for the dead , that I might look upon him before burial , since I had never seen him . Then , besides , theirs was a fine promise , if by accompanying them I might sack the towers of Troy .
It was now the second day of my voyage

sections.label: Section 355
sections.original: κἀγὼ πικρὸν Σίγειον οὐρίῳ πλάτῃ
sections.translation:
when , sped by breeze and oar , I approached bitter Sigeum . When I landed , straightaway the entire army thronged around me with greetings , vowing that they saw their lost Achilles once more alive .
He , though , lay ready for burial , and I , unhappy ,

sections.label: Section 360
sections.original: ἐπεὶ ʼδάκρυσα κεῖνον , οὐ μακρῷ χρόνῳ
sections.translation: when I had wept for him , went before long to the Atreids , to friends , as it was reasonable to suppose ,— and claimed my father ’ s arms and all else that had been his . O , their reply was bold and shameless ! Seed of Achilles , you may take all else that was your father ’ s . But of those arms another man now is lord , the son of Laertes .

sections.label: Section 367
sections.original: κἀγὼ δακρύσας εὐθὺς ἐξανίσταμαι
sections.translation: The tears came quick to my eyes as I sprang up in passionate anger and said in my bitterness , Madman ! Have you dared give my arms to another man in my place , without asking me ? But Odysseus — for he chanced to be at hand — said , Yes , boy , they awarded them as was just , since it was I who saved the arms and their master by my presence at the crucial moment . Then immediately , in my fury , I began to lash at him with every kind of insult

sections.label: Section 375
sections.original: τοῖς πᾶσιν , οὐδὲν ἐνδεὲς ποιούμενος ,
sections.translation: and left not one unsaid , if he was indeed to rob me of my arms . At this point , stung by the abuse , though not given to anger , he answered ,— You have not gone to where we have ; instead you have been absent from where you were needed . And since your tongue is so arrogant , you will never sail back to Scyros with those arms in your possession . In that way rebuked , in that way insulted , I sail for home , deprived of what is my own by that worst offspring of a wicked line , Odysseus .

sections.label: Section 385
sections.original: κοὐκ αἰτιῶμαι κεῖνον ὡς τοὺς ἐν τέλει ·
sections.translation: And yet I do not blame him as much as I do those in power . For a city hangs wholly on its leaders , and so does an army , but when men shatter law and order , it is the lessons of their teachers that corrupt them . My tale is told in full . May he who hates the Atreids

sections.label: Section 390
sections.original: ἐμοί θʼ ὁμοίως καὶ θεοῖς εἴη φίλος .
sections.translation: be as dear to the gods as he is to me !

sections.label: Section 1.391
sections.original: ὀρεστέρα παμβῶτι Γᾶ , μᾶτερ αὐτοῦ Διός ,
sections.translation: Goddess of the hills , Earth all-nourishing , mother of Zeus himself , you through whose realm the great Pactolus

sections.label: Section 1.395
sections.original: σὲ κἀκεῖ , μᾶτερ πότνιʼ , ἐπηυδώμαν ,
sections.translation: rolls golden sands ! There , there also , dread Mother , I called upon your name , when all the insults of the Atreids landed upon this man , when they handed over his father ’ s armor , that sublime marvel ,

sections.label: Section 1.400
sections.original: ἰὼ μάκαιρα ταυροκτόνων
sections.translation: to the son of Laertes . Hear it , blessed queen , who rides on bull-slaughtering lions !

sections.label: Section 403
sections.original: ἔχοντες , ὡς ἔοικε , σύμβολον σαφὲς
sections.translation: It seems that you have come to me , friends , well commended by a grief that matches my own .

sections.label: Section 405
sections.original: καί μοι προσᾴδεθʼ ὥστε γιγνώσκειν ὅτι
sections.translation: Your story is in harmony with mine , so that I can recognize the work of the Atreids and of Odysseus . For well I know that he would put his tongue to any base tale and to any mischief-making , if thereby he could hope to accomplish something criminal in the end .

sections.label: Section 410
sections.original: ἀλλʼ οὔ τι τοῦτο θαῦμʼ ἔμοιγʼ , ἀλλʼ εἰ παρὼν
sections.translation: No , that is not at all a wonder to me , but rather that the elder Ajax , if he was there , could bear to see this .

sections.label: Section 412
sections.original: οὐκ ἦν ἔτι ζῶν , ὦ ξένʼ · οὐ γὰρ ἄν ποτε
sections.translation: Ah , friend , he was no longer alive — I would never have been plundered like that while he lived .

sections.label: Section 414
sections.original: πῶς εἶπας ; ἀλλʼ ἦ χοὖτος οἴχεται θανών ;
sections.translation: What do you say ? Is he , too , dead and gone ?

sections.label: Section 415
sections.original: ὡς μηκέτʼ ὄντα κεῖνον ἐν φάει νόει .
sections.translation: Think of him as of one who sees the sun ’ s light no more .

sections.label: Section 416
sections.original: οἴμοι τάλας . ἀλλʼ οὐχ ὁ Τυδέως γόνος
sections.translation: Oh , no ! But the son of Tydeus , and Sisyphus ’ offspring that was bought by Laertes — they will not die , since they do not deserve to live !

sections.label: Section 419
sections.original: οὐ δῆτʼ · ἐπίστω τοῦτό γʼ · ἀλλὰ καὶ μέγα
sections.translation: No , indeed , be sure of it . On the contrary , they prosper now

sections.label: Section 420
sections.original: θάλλοντές εἰσι νῦν ἐν Ἀργείων στρατῷ .
sections.translation: — yes , and greatly — in the Argive army .

sections.label: Section 421
sections.original: τί δʼ ; οὐ παλαιὸς κἀγαθὸς φίλος τʼ ἐμός ,
sections.translation: And what of my brave old friend , Nestor of Pylos — is he not alive ? He often checked the crimes of those two , if not others , by his sage counsels .

sections.label: Section 424
sections.original: κεῖνός γε πράσσει νῦν κακῶς , ἐπεὶ θανὼν
sections.translation: He has his own troubles now , since Antilochus ,

sections.label: Section 425
sections.original: Ἀντίλοχος αὐτῷ φροῦδος , ὃς παρῆν , γόνος .
sections.translation: the son that was at his side , left him for Hades .

sections.label: Section 426
sections.original: οἴμοι , δύʼ αὖ τώδʼ ἄνδρʼ ἔλεξας , οἷν ἐγὼ
sections.translation: Ah , me ! These two , again , whom you have named , are men of whose death I had least wished to hear . Gods ! What are we to look for , when these men have died , but Odysseus here again lives , when

sections.label: Section 430
sections.original: χρῆν ἀντὶ τούτων αὐτὸν αὐδᾶσθαι νεκρόν ;
sections.translation: in their place he should have been announced as dead ?

sections.label: Section 431
sections.original: σοφὸς παλαιστὴς κεῖνος · ἀλλὰ χαἰ σοφαὶ
sections.translation: The man is a clever wrestler . But even clever schemes , Philoctetes , are often blocked .

sections.label: Section 433
sections.original: φέρʼ εἰπὲ πρὸς θεῶν , ποῦ γὰρ ἦν ἐνταῦθά σοι
sections.translation: Now , by the gods , tell me — where was Patroclus when you needed him , he whom your father loved beyond all others ?

sections.label: Section 435
sections.original: χοὖτος τεθνηκὼς ἦν · λόγῳ δέ σʼ ἐν βραχεῖ
sections.translation: He , too , was dead . And in a brief maxim I would teach you this : War takes no evil man by choice , but always the good men .

sections.label: Section 438
sections.original: ξυμμαρτυρῶ σοι · καὶ κατʼ αὐτὸ τοῦτό γε
sections.translation: I will attest to that , and with that very truth in mind , I will ask you how fares a man of little worth , but sharp of tongue and clever .

sections.label: Section 440
sections.original: γλώσσῃ δὲ δεινοῦ καὶ σοφοῦ , τί νῦν κυρεῖ .
sections.translation: Surely the man of whom you ask is no one but Odysseus ?

sections.label: Section 441
sections.original: ποίου δὲ τούτου πλήν γʼ Ὀδυσσέως ἐρεῖς ;
sections.translation: I did not mean him ; there was one Thersites , who could never be content to speak once and briefly , even though no one wanted him to speak at all . Do you know if he is alive ?

sections.label: Section 445
sections.original: οὐκ εἶδον αὐτόν , ᾐσθόμην δʼ ἔτʼ ὄντα νιν .
sections.translation: I never saw him , but I heard that he is still alive .

sections.label: Section 446
sections.original: ἔμελλʼ · ἐπεὶ οὐδέν πω κακόν γʼ ἀπώλετο ,
sections.translation: He would be — no evil thing has ever been known to perish . No , the gods take excellent care of their kind . They find a strange joy in turning back from Hades all things criminal

sections.label: Section 450
sections.original: δίκαια καὶ τὰ χρήστʼ ἀποστέλλουσʼ ἀεί .
sections.translation: and crooked , while they are always dispatching the just and the good from life . How am I to regard these doings ? How can I praise them , when in the very act of praising the ways of the gods , I find that the gods are evil ?

sections.label: Section 453
sections.original: ἐγὼ μέν , ὦ γένεθλον Οἰταίου πατρός ,
sections.translation: I , at least , son of Oetean Poeas , will be on my guard hereafter against Ilium

sections.label: Section 455
sections.original: καὶ τοὺς Ἀτρείδας εἰσορῶν φυλάξομαι ·
sections.translation: and the Atreids , and look on them only from afar . And where the worse man is stronger than the good , where nobility goes to ruin and the vile man dominates — among such men I will never make my friends . No , rocky Scyros shall suffice for me

sections.label: Section 460
sections.original: ἔσται τὸ λοιπόν , ὥστε τέρπεσθαι δόμῳ .
sections.translation: from now on to make me delight in my home . Now to my ship ! And you , son of Poeas , farewell — as best you can , farewell ! May the gods free you of your disease , just as you wish ! But we must be going , so that we

sections.label: Section 465
sections.original: πλοῦν ἡμὶν εἴκῃ , τηνικαῦθʼ ὁρμώμεθα .
sections.translation: may set sail whenever the god permits our voyage .

sections.label: Section 466
sections.original: ἤδη , τέκνον , στέλλεσθε ;
sections.translation: Are you setting out already , son ?

sections.label: Section 466a
sections.original: καιρὸς γὰρ καλεῖ
sections.translation: Yes , since opportunity bids us watch near our ship for a fair wind , rather than from afar .

sections.label: Section 468
sections.original: πρός νύν σε πατρὸς πρός τε μητρός , ὦ τέκνον ,
sections.translation: Now by your father and by your mother , son , by all that you cherish at home —

sections.label: Section 470
sections.original: ἱκέτης ἱκνοῦμαι , μὴ λίπῃς μʼ οὕτω μόνον ,
sections.translation: I solemnly supplicate you , do not leave me alone like this , helpless amid these miseries in which I live , so harsh as you see , and so numerous as I have said ! Consider me a small side-task . Great is

sections.label: Section 475
sections.original: ὅμως δὲ τλῆθι · τοῖσι γενναίοισί τοι
sections.translation: your disgust , well I know , at such a cargo . Yet bear with it all the same — to noble minds baseness is hateful , and a good deed is glorious . If you forsake this task , you will have a stain on your honor ; but if you perform it , boy , you will win the prize of highest honor — if I return alive to Oeta ’ s soil .

sections.label: Section 480
sections.original: ἴθʼ · ἡμέρας τοι μόχθος οὐχ ὅλης μιᾶς .
sections.translation: Come , the trouble will not last one full day . Endure it , take me and throw me where you will — in the hold , the prow , the stern , wherever I will least annoy my shipmates . Say yes , by the great god of suppliants , son ;
